column6: Classroom teacher, Supply / peripatetic, Support staff, School leader, Student / trainee
column8: The largest education union in the UK and indeed Europe. Came about as a merger between the National Union of Teachers and Association of Teachers and Lecturers in 2017. It is known for national campaigning, collective action, a strong activist culture and an extensive network of school representatives. Covers teachers, leaders and support staff across England, Wales and Northern Ireland. Affiliated to the TUC.
column9:
Largest education union — strongest collective voice

column10:

Representation quality may vary locally with speed of support depending on the strength of the local branch.
Considered by GMB, Unison and Unite to have gone against a TUC agreement to not recruit support staff

column6: Classroom teacher, Supply / peripatetic, School leader, Student / trainee
column8: A specialist teachers' union that has roots dating back to 1976 and has entities covering all four UK nations. Focuses on workplace rights, pay and conditions. It has often been seen as less broad-based than the NEU because it represents qualified teachers rather than the wider education workforce, but it remains willing to campaign and take industrial action where it believes members’ interests require it.
column9:

Perceived as more teacher-profession focused
Active across England, Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland
Strong emphasis on pay, workload and conditions
Large union with over 250,000 members

Struggled to meet minimum ballot thresholds for previous national industrial action votes

column6: Classroom teacher, Supply / peripatetic, Support staff, School leader, Student / trainee
column8: Relatively modern offering compared to traditional unions as only founded in 2012. Styles itself as the apolitical alternative to a teaching union. For those who want the type of support and advice that comes from education unions but are not intersted in the wider campaigning and politics. Only operates in England and Wales. Focuses on supporting members as individuals rather than as collective groups. Supports anybody working in a school environment including support staff.

Whilst they can't vote in other unions' ballots, members can still join strikes as non-union participants if they want to have their voice heard
Cheapest membership cost in most instances as no fees spent on campaigning etc
Public reviews of its support are excellent
column10:

column2: Association of School and College Leaders
column8: It describes itself as representing more than 25,000 leaders across primary, secondary, post-16, college and trust settings, and says it has no political affiliation. ASCL is often perceived as policy-focused and professionally oriented, with a strong emphasis on national education policy, leadership advice, member support and professional development. Covers England, Wales and Northern Ireland. History dating back to 1874.

column8: A trade union and professional association for school leaders, particularly headteachers, deputy heads, assistant heads, school business leaders and other senior leaders. It represents more than 28,500 members across England, Wales and Northern Ireland, with membership spanning early years, primary, special, secondary, independent, sixth form, FE and other educational settings. It tends to focus on leadership conditions, accountability, inspection, funding, workload, recruitment and the practical realities of running schools. It is affiliated to the TUC. Long history going back to 1897.
